body_txt: The Union of Soviet Socialist Republics ( USSR ), the United States ' sworn enemy during the Cold War , has gone through several periods of stereotyping. The most famous example is Communist Russia during the Cold War era: everyone in red , calling each other "comrade" and supposedly threatening the world's freedom in the name of Communism . With the fall of Communism in the country, this one is pretty much dead . Still, Vladimir Putin did rise to prominence through the KGB . Then there are tall furry hats, That Russian Squat Dance , borscht , vodka ( lots of vodka ), and everyone constantly being miserable and cold , which is why they drink the vodka . These tropes seem permanently associated with the area. In recent years, the Russian bride has started to turn up a lot. Chernobyl has also become very important in the outside world's views of the country, even though it's in Ukraine . Saint Basil's Cathedral ◊ will probably show up, too. No, it's not the Kremlin . This ◊ is the Kremlin note Moscow Kremlin, to be precise. There are actually many others . That church next door is the Cathedral of the Annunciation. (image searches on "Kremlin" invariably turn up far more of the former building than the latter). For obvious reasons , the Sovetskiy Soyuz (and later Rossiyskaya Federatsiya ) have been subject to a lot of California Doubling over the years, although there are a number of late eighties films (such as The Russia House ) actually filmed in Moscow, because of glasnost . This article will list some of the more common ways for foreigners to get all things Russian wrong. In Russia proper, they're called razvesistaya klyukva (развесистая клюква, "tall tales", literally "blooming cranberries") or just klyukva (клюква, "cranberry") and are a source of much humor .  See Chummy Commies , Dirty Communists , Heroic Russian Émigré , Husky Russkie , Make the Bear Angry Again , Mother Russia Makes You Strong , and Renegade Russian for more stereotypes about Russians. For Useful Notes about Russia and its predecessors in some depth, see here and here .
